era_context: The year 668 CE marks a pivotal moment in East Asian history with the fall of Goguryeo, one of the Three Kingdoms of Korea. Simultaneously, the Byzantine Empire faces increasing Arab pressure in the Mediterranean, and the Umayyad Caliphate consolidates power under Muawiyah I. The Tang Dynasty reaches the height of its territorial expansion.

events.title: Fall of Goguryeo; Tang Dynasty conquest of Korean peninsula
events.description: After a prolonged military campaign lasting several years, the Tang Dynasty under Emperor Gaozong defeats and conquers the kingdom of Goguryeo, one of the Three Kingdoms of Korea. The fall of Goguryeo marks the end of an independent Korean state and the establishment of Tang hegemony over the peninsula. The conquest is followed by the rise of Silla, which eventually unifies Korea under Tang suzerainty. This event represents the peak of Tang territorial expansion and establishes Chinese dominance in East Asia for decades.

sources.name: Samguk Sagi (History of the Three Kingdoms, Korean source)
Historiographic consensus from East Asian scholarship

events.certainty_note: The fall of Goguryeo in 668 CE is one of the most well-documented events of the 7th century, attested in both Chinese and Korean sources. The date is certain.

events.title: Muawiyah I consolidates Umayyad Caliphate; moves capital to Damascus
events.description: Muawiyah I, the founder of the Umayyad Caliphate, consolidates his power and establishes Damascus as the capital of the Islamic state. By 668 CE, Muawiyah has defeated his rivals (including Ali and his successors) and established a stable, centralized caliphate. The move to Damascus, a former Byzantine city, symbolizes the Umayyad appropriation of Byzantine administrative structures and the shift of Islamic power from the Hijaz to the Levant. This administrative reorganization enables the Umayyad expansion into North Africa and the Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Muawiyah I's consolidation of power and establishment of Damascus as capital are well-attested in Islamic sources. The process occurred over several years, but by 668 CE, his authority is firmly established.

events.title: Byzantine-Arab naval conflict in the Mediterranean
events.description: Byzantine and Arab naval forces clash in the Mediterranean as the Umayyad Caliphate expands its maritime power. These conflicts precede the formal siege of Constantinople (670–677 CE) and demonstrate the growing Arab naval capability. Byzantine control of the Mediterranean is increasingly challenged, though the empire retains significant naval strength.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts in the late 660s are attested, but specific engagements in 668 are not clearly documented. The year is assigned within the broader conflict period.

events.title: Reign of Emperor Gaozong of Tang; consolidation of Tang power
events.description: Emperor Gaozong of Tang (r. 649–683) continues to consolidate Tang power and expand the empire's territorial reach. By 668 CE, with the conquest of Goguryeo, the Tang Dynasty reaches its maximum territorial extent. Gaozong's reign is marked by military expansion, administrative reforms, and the gradual rise of his consort Wu Zetian's political influence. The Tang Dynasty is at the height of its power and prestige.
events.certainty_note: Gaozong's reign and the Tang Dynasty's expansion are well-documented. The year 668 marks the conquest of Goguryeo, a confirmed event.

disconfirming_evidence: The fall of Goguryeo is sometimes dated to 667 CE in some sources, though 668 CE is the scholarly consensus. Muawiyah I's consolidation of power occurred gradually over the 660s; the year 668 does not mark a specific turning point, though it is a convenient date for marking his established authority. Byzantine-Arab naval conflicts are attested for the late 660s, but 668 is not specifically distinguished.
historiographic_note: The year 668 CE is well-documented for East Asian history, particularly the fall of Goguryeo, which is attested in both Chinese and Korean sources. Islamic and Byzantine sources provide good coverage of Muawiyah I's consolidation and Mediterranean conflicts, though specific 668 events are sometimes conflated with adjacent years. Chinese sources are rich for Tang Dynasty events. The historical record for 668 is relatively strong compared to adjacent years, particularly for East Asia.

graph_edges.note: The fall of Goguryeo leads to Silla's unification of Korea
Muawiyah's consolidated power enables the siege of Constantinople
Gaozong's reign and Wu Zetian's rise lead to her proclamation as Empress
